prevalence.source: Orphanet; European skeletal dysplasia epidemiology reviews
prevalence.trend: stable; high diagnostic confidence and long-term multidisciplinary follow-up

burden.dalyPerCapita: very low (rare) but significant lifelong morbidity (ENT, orthopedic, neurologic)
burden.mortalityRate: Low; higher in infancy for severe airway/foramen magnum complications

vulnerablePopulations: children with sleep apnea and recurrent otitis
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical-radiographic diagnosis with FGFR3 confirmation; structured surveillance for neurologic compression, hydrocephalus, sleep apnea and hearing problems

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; early clinical diagnosis common
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Tens of thousands across the EU (rare)
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Good with access to multidisciplinary clinics and timely complication management

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Genetics confirmation widely available; multidisciplinary skeletal dysplasia clinics in many countries
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Orthopedic alignment and spine care, neurosurgical assessment for foramen magnum stenosis, ENT/hearing care, sleep apnea evaluation, physiotherapy and genetic counseling
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Variation in access to specialized dysplasia clinics across countries
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Strong multidisciplinary pathways and rehabilitative services; high availability of imaging and genetics
